# Training & Fine-tuning

## Unsloth

**URL:** https://unsloth.ai
**Repository:** https://github.com/unslothai/unsloth
**llms.txt:** https://docs.unsloth.ai/llms.txt (published)
**License:** open-source  |  **Deployment:** library  |  Platforms: linux, windows
**Tags:** fine-tuning, training, lora, qlora, unsloth, triton, python

*2x faster LLM fine-tuning with 70% less memory — drop-in replacement for HuggingFace's training stack.*

Unsloth is a fine-tuning library that rewrites HuggingFace's training
code with hand-tuned Triton kernels and careful memory management to
achieve roughly 2x speedups and 70% memory savings versus vanilla
Transformers + PEFT. It supports LoRA, QLoRA, full fine-tuning, and
continued pretraining across Llama, Qwen, Mistral, Gemma, Phi, and most
modern architectures.

The project is known for free, working Colab notebooks that take users
from zero to a fine-tuned model in under an hour — genuine entry-point
material for the LLM fine-tuning community. It's compatible with the
HuggingFace ecosystem: PEFT adapters, Trainer API, TRL DPO, datasets,
and every output format (LoRA, merged, GGUF, AWQ) downstream tools
expect.

A Pro tier adds multi-GPU training and priority support.

**Features:**
- 2x faster LoRA and QLoRA fine-tuning vs vanilla Transformers
- 70% memory reduction through careful gradient management
- Support for Llama, Qwen, Mistral, Gemma, Phi, and more
- LoRA, QLoRA, full fine-tuning, continued pretraining
- DPO, ORPO, KTO via TRL integration
- Export to GGUF, AWQ, merged, or LoRA-only formats
- Free Colab notebooks for common fine-tuning recipes

**Use cases:**
- Fine-tuning LLMs on consumer GPUs (24GB and below)
- Cost-effective domain adaptation for internal applications
- Teaching / learning fine-tuning with working starter notebooks
- Rapid iteration on fine-tunes before scaling to multi-GPU
- Reducing training costs on cloud GPU providers

---

## Axolotl

**URL:** https://axolotl.ai
**Repository:** https://github.com/axolotl-ai-cloud/axolotl
**llms.txt:** https://docs.axolotl.ai/llms.txt (missing)
**License:** open-source  |  **Deployment:** library  |  Platforms: linux
**Tags:** fine-tuning, training, axolotl, lora, dpo, yaml, python

*YAML-configured fine-tuning framework supporting LoRA, QLoRA, full FT, DPO, and most modern LLM architectures.*

Axolotl is a full-featured open-source fine-tuning framework that wraps
HuggingFace Transformers, PEFT, and TRL into a YAML-configured CLI.
Users define their training run (model, dataset, hyperparameters,
adapter type) in a config file and launch with `accelerate launch -m
axolotl.cli.train config.yml`.

Compared to Unsloth's speed focus, Axolotl prioritizes breadth: broader
architecture support (Llama, Qwen, Mistral, Gemma, Phi, DeepSeek, Yi,
Cohere, StableLM, and more), more training methods (LoRA, QLoRA, full
FT, ReLoRA, continued pretraining, DPO, KTO, ORPO, GRPO), and richer
dataset formats (SharePT, Alpaca, ChatML, raw, completion, many more).

It's a common choice for production fine-tuning pipelines where
reproducibility and config-as-code matter more than squeezing the last
percent of speed.

**Features:**
- YAML-configured training runs (no code required for common recipes)
- Supports 20+ base model architectures
- LoRA, QLoRA, full FT, ReLoRA, DPO, KTO, ORPO, GRPO
- Multi-GPU via Accelerate and DeepSpeed
- FlashAttention and sample packing for efficiency
- Direct integration with WandB and MLflow for tracking
- Docker images for reproducible training environments

**Use cases:**
- Production fine-tuning pipelines with config-as-code
- Multi-GPU / multi-node training runs
- Preference tuning via DPO, KTO, ORPO, GRPO
- Continued pretraining on domain corpora
- Teams wanting reproducible training via version-controlled YAMLs

---

# Deployment & Hosting

## RunPod

**URL:** https://runpod.io
**llms.txt:** https://docs.runpod.io/llms.txt (published)
**License:** commercial  |  **Deployment:** saas  |  Platforms: linux, macos, windows
**Tags:** gpu-cloud, runpod, serverless, inference, training, deployment

*GPU cloud platform with on-demand instances, serverless endpoints, and a community GPU marketplace — priced for AI workloads.*

RunPod is a GPU cloud provider focused on AI workloads. It offers
three deployment modes: Pods (long-running GPU instances, similar to
bare-metal rentals), Serverless (auto-scaling API endpoints that bill
per-second of inference), and Secure Cloud vs Community Cloud (the
latter being excess capacity from third-party hosts at lower prices,
with the tradeoff of less guaranteed availability).

Its pricing is consistently among the lowest for consumer and
enterprise GPUs (RTX 4090, A100, H100, H200, L4, L40S), and the
Serverless tier makes it practical to host inference APIs without
paying for idle GPU time. Pre-built templates cover common AI stacks
(ComfyUI, Automatic1111, vLLM, SGLang, Ollama), so new instances can
launch with a running application in one click.

RunPod is a common pick for indie AI builders, researchers, and
teams running bursty inference where Modal / Replicate's managed
layer isn't needed.

**Features:**
- Pods (long-running GPU instances) and Serverless endpoints
- Community Cloud for lowest-cost GPU rentals
- {'Pre-built templates': 'ComfyUI, vLLM, Ollama, SGLang, Stable Diffusion'}
- Consumer (RTX 3090/4090/5090) and enterprise (A100/H100/H200) GPUs
- Per-second billing on Serverless
- Network volume storage persistent across pods
- API and CLI for programmatic pod / endpoint management
- Global datacenters for latency-sensitive workloads

**Use cases:**
- GPU workloads priced lower than AWS / GCP / Azure
- Serverless hosting for custom fine-tuned models
- Running ComfyUI or Stable Diffusion at scale without local GPU
- Research and experimentation on latest consumer/enterprise GPUs
- Burst capacity for training or batch inference jobs
- Deploying vLLM / SGLang behind a stable endpoint

---

## xAI

**URL:** https://x.ai
**llms.txt:** https://docs.x.ai/llms.txt (published)
**License:** commercial  |  **Deployment:** saas  |  Platforms: linux, macos, windows
**Tags:** llm-provider, xai, grok, api, openai-compatible, value, reasoning

*xAI's Grok API — Grok 4.1 Fast Reasoning and Non-reasoning currently the best raw-intelligence-per-dollar offering on the market.*

xAI provides hosted inference and training for the Grok family of
models via an OpenAI-compatible API. Its 2026 lineup includes Grok 4
(flagship reasoning model), Grok 4.1 Fast Reasoning, and Grok 4.1
Fast Non-reasoning — the "Fast" variants being the standout for
application developers on price-performance grounds.

**Grok 4.1 Fast pricing crushed the cheap-model market when it
launched.** At a fraction of GPT-5-mini, Claude Haiku 4.1, or Gemini
2.5 Flash pricing, and with measurably stronger reasoning on hard
benchmarks, it became the default "high value per token" option for
agent workloads running millions of tokens per day. If you're
routing by cost-per-quality, Grok 4.1 Fast Reasoning is almost
always in the candidate set.

One architectural note: reasoning is baked into the model ID on xAI,
not a runtime parameter. You pick `grok-4-1-fast-reasoning` for
chain-of-thought behavior or `grok-4-1-fast-non-reasoning` for
straight completion — unlike Anthropic or OpenAI where reasoning is
a flag on a single model endpoint. Budget accordingly.

The API is OpenAI-compatible for ergonomics: drop-in for existing
OpenAI-SDK code with a `base_url` change, streaming supported, tool
use supported, long context windows (256k+ on Grok 4 class). Vision,
live web search via Grok's in-product search index, and batch
inference are also exposed.

**Features:**
- OpenAI-compatible API — drop-in replacement by base_url
- Grok 4 (flagship), Grok 4.1 Fast Reasoning, Grok 4.1 Fast Non-reasoning
- Exceptional price-performance on the "Fast" variants
- Long context windows (256k+ tokens on Grok 4 tier)
- Tool use / function calling, streaming, structured outputs
- Live web search integration via Grok's in-product index
- Vision support on flagship models
- Batch API for asynchronous high-volume jobs

**Use cases:**
- High-volume agent loops where cost-per-token compounds fast
- Reasoning-heavy tasks where Grok 4.1 Fast beats pricier alternatives
- Coding assistants and tool-using agents on a budget
- Replacing GPT-5-mini / Haiku / Gemini Flash as the "default cheap model"
- Applications needing a Grok-specific capability (live web search)
- Multi-provider fallback stacks where xAI is one of the candidates

---

## Fireworks AI

**URL:** https://fireworks.ai
**llms.txt:** https://docs.fireworks.ai/llms.txt (missing)
**License:** commercial  |  **Deployment:** saas  |  Platforms: linux, macos, windows
**Tags:** inference, fireworks, serverless, fast, openai-compatible, fine-tuning

*Production inference platform for open-source models with industry-leading speed for DeepSeek, Llama, Qwen.*

Fireworks AI is a production inference platform specializing in
serving open-source LLMs at low latency and high throughput. Its
inference stack (in-house optimized, built on FireAttention) regularly
benchmarks as the fastest provider for DeepSeek-R1 / V3, Llama 3/4,
Qwen 3, and other large open-weight models.

Beyond serverless inference, Fireworks offers dedicated deployments
(guaranteed capacity, custom models, SLA), fine-tuning as a managed
service, and agent-oriented features like structured outputs, function
calling, and JSON mode across every hosted model.

It's a common pick for AI products that need open-weight models in
production with strict latency requirements — voice agents, real-time
coding assistants, high-QPS chat applications.

**Features:**
- Optimized inference stack with industry-leading tokens/sec benchmarks
- 100+ open-source models (DeepSeek, Llama, Qwen, Mixtral, Mistral)
- OpenAI-compatible API with streaming, tools, JSON mode
- Fine-tuning as a service with managed serving
- Dedicated deployments for guaranteed capacity and SLAs
- {'Multimodal': 'text, vision, image generation, embedding, speech'}
- Per-million-tokens and per-GPU-hour pricing tiers

**Use cases:**
- Production applications with strict latency requirements
- Real-time AI products (voice agents, live coding, streaming chat)
- Serving fine-tuned DeepSeek or Llama variants at scale
- Migrating off OpenAI / Anthropic for cost reasons
- Multi-model inference behind a single bill and dashboard

---

## Groq

**URL:** https://groq.com
**llms.txt:** https://console.groq.com/llms.txt (missing)
**License:** commercial  |  **Deployment:** saas  |  Platforms: linux, macos, windows
**Tags:** inference, lpu, groq, saas, low-latency, openai-compatible

*Ultra-low-latency LLM inference on custom LPU silicon — sub-second complete responses and OpenAI-compatible API.*

Groq operates a cloud inference service built on its own custom "Language
Processing Unit" (LPU) silicon, designed specifically for transformer
inference. The result is inference speeds measured in hundreds of tokens
per second for models like Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek, and Kimi — fast enough
that full multi-paragraph responses complete before a human reader can
finish the first sentence.

The API is OpenAI-compatible, so any application using the OpenAI client
can point at Groq's endpoint with a base URL change. Pricing is
per-million-tokens and competitive with other serverless inference
providers, with the key differentiator being latency.

Groq is a common pick for real-time applications: voice agents, live
coding assistants, streaming chat UIs, and any use case where response
latency (not token throughput) is the bottleneck.

**Features:**
- Custom LPU silicon purpose-built for LLM inference
- 300-800+ tokens/sec on popular open-weight models
- OpenAI-compatible API with streaming and tool use
- Function calling and structured output support
- {'Model catalog': 'Llama 3/4, Qwen, DeepSeek, Kimi, Gemma, Whisper'}
- Free tier with generous rate limits for prototyping
- Batch API for async high-volume jobs

**Use cases:**
- Real-time voice agents and conversational AI
- Streaming chat UIs where time-to-first-token matters
- Agent loops with many sequential LLM calls (speed compounds)
- Live coding assistants (inline autocomplete, fast chat)
- High-throughput inference pipelines with latency SLOs

---

## Modal

**URL:** https://modal.com
**llms.txt:** https://modal.com/docs/llms.txt (missing)
**License:** commercial  |  **Deployment:** saas  |  Platforms: linux, macos, windows
**Tags:** serverless, gpu, python, modal, inference, training, saas

*Serverless cloud platform for Python with first-class GPU support — deploy LLMs, training jobs, and batch pipelines from code.*

Modal is a serverless compute platform designed for Python workloads
that need GPUs: LLM inference, model training, batch image generation,
data processing, and long-running AI pipelines. You write functions in
Python with decorators that specify container image, GPU type, memory,
and schedule — Modal handles the rest: cold starts in seconds, billing
per-second, auto-scaling to zero when idle.

The developer experience is the differentiator. Locally-written Python
functions deploy to cloud GPUs without leaving the editor: `modal run
my_script.py` executes on an A100 or H100, `modal deploy` exposes it as
an HTTPS endpoint. Popular use cases include hosting vLLM or SGLang for
inference, running Unsloth fine-tuning jobs, and dispatching ComfyUI
workflows at scale.

Modal is used heavily by AI startups that want cloud GPUs without
managing Kubernetes, and by teams that need bursty capacity without
reserving hardware.

**Features:**
- Serverless Python functions on cloud GPUs (A100, H100, L4, L40S, …)
- Sub-10-second cold starts with container snapshotting
- {'Auto-scaling': 'scale to zero when idle, scale up under load'}
- Per-second billing, no reserved capacity required
- Built-in web endpoints, scheduled jobs, and WebSocket support
- Volumes, secrets, and distributed dicts for state
- CPU-only functions also supported for pipeline orchestration

**Use cases:**
- Serverless LLM inference hosting (vLLM, SGLang, TGI)
- Fine-tuning jobs that need a GPU for a few hours, then shut down
- Batch image / video generation with ComfyUI or diffusers
- AI application backends where traffic is spiky
- Replacing Kubernetes + GPU nodes for AI workloads
- Webhooks, scheduled jobs, and event-driven AI pipelines

---

## Replicate

**URL:** https://replicate.com
**llms.txt:** https://replicate.com/docs/llms.txt (missing)
**License:** commercial  |  **Deployment:** saas  |  Platforms: linux, macos, windows
**Tags:** inference, replicate, api, open-source-models, saas, cog

*Run thousands of open-source ML models via simple API calls — image, video, audio, text — with per-second billing.*

Replicate is a cloud platform that hosts open-source ML models behind
a unified REST API. Thousands of public models — Stable Diffusion,
Flux, SDXL, LLaMA, Whisper, MusicGen, video generation, speech
synthesis, CLIP embeddings, vision models, voice cloning — are
immediately runnable without provisioning GPUs.

Its differentiator is breadth over depth: where Groq and Fireworks
optimize for a curated set of LLMs, Replicate covers the whole
open-source ML landscape, including image / video / audio models that
inference-only LLM platforms don't touch. Pricing is per-second of GPU
time.

Replicate also hosts custom model deploys (package your code + weights
into a Cog container, push, get an endpoint) — useful for teams that
want Replicate's serving infrastructure for their own fine-tuned or
private models.

**Features:**
- Unified REST API across thousands of open-source models
- {'Coverage': 'image, video, audio, text, vision, embeddings'}
- {'Cog': 'containerize your own model and deploy to Replicate'}
- Per-second GPU billing, scale-to-zero
- Webhooks for long-running inference
- Python, JavaScript, Go, Elixir, Swift clients
- Integrations with LangChain, LlamaIndex, Vercel AI SDK

**Use cases:**
- Building apps that need multiple model types (text + image + audio)
- Creative tools — image generation, voice synthesis, video
- Hosting custom fine-tuned models without infrastructure
- Prototyping with many different models before committing to one
- Applications that can tolerate cold-start latencies (~10-30 seconds)

---

## Together AI

**URL:** https://www.together.ai
**llms.txt:** https://docs.together.ai/llms.txt (missing)
**License:** commercial  |  **Deployment:** saas  |  Platforms: linux, macos, windows
**Tags:** inference, together, serverless, openai-compatible, fine-tuning

*Serverless inference for 200+ open-source models with OpenAI-compatible API — low latency, competitive pricing.*

Together AI is a serverless inference platform for open-source LLMs.
It hosts 200+ models including the Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek, Mistral,
Gemma, and Mixtral families, along with image models (FLUX, SD3) and
embedding models — all behind a unified OpenAI-compatible API.

Together's positioning is "run open-source at production speed without
operating GPUs." Its inference stack is tuned on top of vLLM and
in-house optimizations, delivering competitive tokens-per-second and
cost-per-million-tokens among the serverless providers (Fireworks,
Groq, DeepInfra, Replicate).

Together also hosts fine-tuning jobs (upload dataset → get a
fine-tuned checkpoint → serve it on the same platform) and dedicated
endpoints for teams needing isolated capacity. Their open-source
`together` Python client and OpenAI compatibility make migration from
OpenAI trivial.

**Features:**
- Serverless inference for 200+ open-source models
- OpenAI-compatible API with streaming, tool use, structured outputs
- Per-million-tokens pricing with tiered rate limits
- Managed fine-tuning (LoRA and full FT)
- Dedicated endpoints for enterprise isolation
- Image generation (FLUX, Stable Diffusion 3)
- Embedding and reranking models

**Use cases:**
- Production applications needing open-source LLMs without GPU ops
- Cost-optimized inference as an OpenAI alternative
- Fine-tuning pipelines with managed training + serving
- Multi-model architectures (chat + embeddings + image in one bill)
- Teams wanting open-weight model access without self-hosting

---

# Desktop Applications

## Claude Desktop

**URL:** https://claude.com/download
**llms.txt:** https://docs.claude.com/llms.txt (published)
**License:** commercial  |  **Deployment:** local  |  Platforms: linux, macos, windows
**Tags:** desktop-app, claude, anthropic, mcp, skills, agent, assistant

*Anthropic's native desktop app for Claude — MCP server support, skills, agent mode, and deep OS integration.*

Claude Desktop is Anthropic's official native application for macOS,
Windows, and Linux. It runs on top of Claude's models (Sonnet, Opus,
Haiku) with features that go well beyond the claude.ai web interface:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) server support for connecting external
tools and data, a skills system for packaging custom workflows, agent
mode for autonomous multi-step tasks, and native OS integrations
(global shortcuts, file uploads, clipboard, voice input on supported
platforms).

For power users, Claude Desktop is the most capable consumer-facing
Claude product: it exposes the full tool-use loop, handles long
conversations, persists chat history locally, and accepts MCP servers
that extend it into nearly any workflow (GitHub, databases, APIs,
browser, local filesystem).

It shares documentation and the underlying API with the Claude Agent
SDK and Claude Code, meaning `llms.txt` at docs.claude.com serves all
three.

**Features:**
- Native apps for macOS, Windows, and Linux
- MCP (Model Context Protocol) server integration
- Skills system for reusable custom workflows
- Agent mode for autonomous multi-step tasks
- Native OS integrations (shortcuts, clipboard, file uploads)
- Long-form chat with local history persistence
- Supports Sonnet, Opus, Haiku model families
- Voice input on supported platforms

**Use cases:**
- Daily AI assistant with access to local files and tools via MCP
- Power-user chat with full tool use (browser, shell, APIs)
- Workflow automation via custom skills
- Research, writing, and coding assistance at the desktop
- Users who want Claude's capabilities beyond the web UI

---

# Operating Systems (AI-capable Linux)

## CachyOS

**URL:** https://cachyos.org
**Repository:** https://github.com/CachyOS
**llms.txt:** https://cachyos.org/llms.txt (missing)
**License:** open-source  |  **Deployment:** local  |  Platforms: linux
**Tags:** linux, arch, cachyos, performance, kernel, ai-workstation

*Arch-based Linux distribution with performance-tuned kernels and a popular choice for local AI / ML workloads.*

CachyOS is a rolling-release Linux distribution built on Arch, focused
on desktop performance through custom-compiled kernels (BORE, EEVDF,
sched-ext), aggressive x86_64-v3 / v4 optimization, and bleeding-edge
userspace packages. It ships with KDE Plasma 6 / GNOME / Hyprland
options and integrates the full AUR for rapid access to AI tooling.

For local AI / ML workflows CachyOS is a common pick because: (a) its
kernels handle sustained GPU/CPU load without scheduler quirks, (b)
mainline packaging of Vulkan, ROCm, CUDA, PyTorch, and ComfyUI is
current within days of upstream releases, and (c) Arch's rolling
model means no dist-upgrade drama when a new PyTorch drops the
minimum Python version.

Downside: Arch-style maintenance is hands-on. Not a good pick for
production servers where stability is more important than
currency.

**Features:**
- Arch-based rolling release with performance-tuned kernel options
- BORE, EEVDF, sched-ext kernel schedulers available out of the box
- x86_64-v3 / v4 optimized packages
- KDE Plasma 6 / GNOME / Hyprland / others at install
- Full AUR access for up-to-date AI tooling
- Current CUDA, ROCm, Vulkan drivers and runtimes
- pacman and AUR helpers (yay, paru) pre-configured

**Use cases:**
- Personal AI / ML development workstations
- Gaming + ML dual-purpose desktops
- btrfs-on-NVMe developer setups with snapshot rollback
- Users who want current AI tooling without waiting for distro freezes
- Arch users who don't want to configure Arch from scratch

---

## Fedora

**URL:** https://fedoraproject.org
**llms.txt:** https://docs.fedoraproject.org/llms.txt (missing)
**License:** open-source  |  **Deployment:** local  |  Platforms: linux
**Tags:** linux, fedora, red-hat, rhel-upstream, cuda, selinux

*Upstream of Red Hat Enterprise Linux — leading edge of Linux features with strong AI / ML packaging and enterprise lineage.*

Fedora is Red Hat's upstream distribution — the testbed where
features, packaging, and tooling that eventually land in RHEL get
proven. For AI / ML developers this means current kernels and GPU
drivers alongside enterprise-grade packaging, SELinux policy, and
an ecosystem that translates cleanly to production RHEL deployments.

Fedora ships recent NVIDIA CUDA, ROCm, PyTorch, TensorFlow, and ONNX
Runtime through RPM Fusion and official repos. Its semi-annual
release cadence (two releases per year, 13 months of support each)
keeps versions current without Arch-level churn. Atomic variants
(Fedora Silverblue, Kinoite) provide immutable desktops suitable
for stable AI workstations.

For teams expecting to deploy on RHEL or CentOS Stream, developing on
Fedora avoids the RHEL subscription cost while preserving compatibility
at the packaging and tooling level.

**Features:**
- Upstream of RHEL — features land here first
- Semi-annual releases with 13-month support windows
- Strong NVIDIA CUDA and ROCm packaging via RPM Fusion
- SELinux enforcing by default (security posture suitable for prod)
- Atomic variants (Silverblue, Kinoite) for immutable desktops
- Podman-first container tooling (rootless, daemonless)
- GNOME as default desktop; KDE, Sway, Xfce spins available

**Use cases:**
- Developers whose production target is RHEL or CentOS Stream
- Teams wanting cutting-edge Linux with enterprise polish
- Regulated environments where SELinux and immutability matter
- Workstations needing balance of current tooling and stability
- Podman-centric container workflows for AI infrastructure

---

## Ubuntu

**URL:** https://ubuntu.com
**llms.txt:** https://ubuntu.com/llms.txt (missing)
**License:** open-source  |  **Deployment:** local  |  Platforms: linux
**Tags:** linux, ubuntu, canonical, lts, cuda, ml-default

*The default Linux distribution for ML / AI development — widest documentation, broadest hardware support, baseline target for most AI tool docs.*

Ubuntu is the most widely used Linux distribution in AI / ML work,
not because it's technically superior but because it's the assumed
baseline. When a README says "tested on Linux," it means Ubuntu.
CUDA installation docs, ROCm setup guides, deep-learning framework
tutorials, and hardware vendor instructions are all written against
Ubuntu first.

For AI developers the appeal is pragmatic: the maximum surface area
of tooling that just works with minimal configuration. NVIDIA driver
installs are first-class, Docker support is deeply tested, cloud
images on AWS / GCP / Azure are Ubuntu-first, and the LTS model
(Long Term Support, 5-year security updates) makes it suitable for
both developer desktops and production servers.

Canonical (the company behind Ubuntu) also offers Ubuntu Pro with
extended security maintenance, FIPS compliance, and enterprise
support — relevant for regulated industries running AI workloads.

**Features:**
- LTS releases with 5-year security support (10-year via Pro)
- NVIDIA driver and CUDA Toolkit pre-packaged and well-tested
- ROCm support for AMD GPUs
- First-tier cloud support (AWS, GCP, Azure, Oracle, IBM)
- Snap packaging for sandboxed app distribution
- Ubuntu Pro for enterprise compliance (FIPS, CIS, USG)
- Default target for most ML framework installation docs

**Use cases:**
- New developers who want AI tooling to "just work"
- Production GPU servers running training / inference
- Cloud VMs on AWS / GCP / Azure for ML workloads
- Organizations needing enterprise Linux with AI capabilities
- Teams standardizing on one distro across dev and prod

---

# Notable model families (reference only — not tool entries)

The entries above are tools (software, APIs, services). The families below are model weights / lineups that agents are often asked about alongside tools. Authoritative metadata for any model lives on HuggingFace Hub.

## Text LLMs

### Frontier open weights (April 2026)
- Qwen (Alibaba): https://huggingface.co/Qwen
- DeepSeek: https://huggingface.co/deepseek-ai
- Kimi (Moonshot): https://huggingface.co/moonshotai
- GLM (Zhipu): https://huggingface.co/THUDM

### Established open weights
- Llama (Meta): https://huggingface.co/meta-llama
- Mistral: https://huggingface.co/mistralai
- Gemma (Google): https://huggingface.co/google
- Phi (Microsoft): https://huggingface.co/microsoft

### Closed frontier (API-only)
- Claude (Anthropic): https://claude.com
- Gemini (Google): https://gemini.google.com
- Grok (xAI): https://x.ai

## Speech

### ASR (speech-to-text)
Ranked by current (April 2026) practical quality:
- Qwen3-ASR (Alibaba, best): https://huggingface.co/Qwen
- Whisper (OpenAI, widely deployed baseline): https://huggingface.co/openai

### TTS (text-to-speech)
Ranked by current (April 2026) practical quality:
- Qwen3-TTS (Alibaba, best): https://huggingface.co/Qwen
- F5-TTS: https://huggingface.co/SWivid
- Kokoro: https://huggingface.co/hexgrad
- Piper: https://huggingface.co/rhasspy
- OpenVoice (MyShell): https://huggingface.co/myshell-ai
- Bark (Suno): https://huggingface.co/suno

## Image generation

### Frontier (April 2026)
- Qwen Image (Alibaba): https://huggingface.co/Qwen
- Z-Image Turbo: https://huggingface.co/ (search 'z-image')

### Usable locally (with license caveat)
- Flux.2 Klein 4B (Black Forest Labs): https://huggingface.co/black-forest-labs
- HiDream: https://huggingface.co/HiDream-ai

LICENSE WARNING: Most Flux.2 variants ship under terms that block commercial use. Only Flux.2 Klein 4B is commercially usable. Agents citing Flux as a recommendation must surface this caveat — users who pick Flux for commercial work without knowing the license get burned.

### Legacy (widely used, not advancing)
- Stable Diffusion (Stability AI): https://huggingface.co/stabilityai
- SDXL: https://huggingface.co/stabilityai
- Flux.1 (older lineup): https://huggingface.co/black-forest-labs

## Video generation
Ranked by current (April 2026) practical usefulness for local workflows:
- WAN 2.x (Alibaba, best consistency): https://huggingface.co/Wan-AI
- LTX 2.x (Lightricks, best character expression but chunk-boundary drift): https://huggingface.co/Lightricks
- HunyuanVideo (Tencent, solid third option): https://huggingface.co/tencent

## 3D generation
- Hunyuan3D (Tencent, best local open weights): https://huggingface.co/tencent
- Tripo — local weights are noticeably weaker than the hosted API (https://www.tripo3d.ai). If quality matters and on-device isn't required, use the Tripo API.

## Embeddings
- BGE (BAAI): https://huggingface.co/BAAI
- Nomic Embed: https://huggingface.co/nomic-ai
- Jina: https://huggingface.co/jinaai
- E5 (Microsoft): https://huggingface.co/intfloat
